what_it_does: A code-grounded readiness assessment that asks whether the Company store can be the single store-of-record for both the raw conversation bedrock (75,374 exchanges) and the generated content layer in a planned merge, then answers it by inspecting the filesystem backend, address grammar, vector storage, projection/relation/mark layers, corpus persistence, and scaling limits.
observations: The live store root is /home/tim/company/.data/store/ on ext4 and uses only FilesystemResolver.
Namespaces include objects, refs, ref_history, meta, memo, graphs, surfaced, locks, vectors, sessions, journeys, chat_threads, plus root-level *.jsonl logs.
Vectors are stored as one JSON file per (item, space) under vectors/, using BAAI/bge-m3 at 1024 dimensions, with 2,186 live vectors.
The address grammar covers run://, cas://, blob://, vec://, ui://, code://, skill://, and context://.
The history projection is described as durable cross-session memory on the corpus and explicitly not episodic-memory.
Corpus records are persisted via put_content, set_ref, and append_event, and the writer refuses records missing session/round/project lineage.
events.jsonl is treated as the corpus index, and list_corpus/find_corpus re-read and re-parse the whole file on every call.
The bedrock's existing vectors are 384-dim (MiniLM/bge-small) while the Company retrieval expects 1024-dim, so re-embedding would be required.
The verdict is architecture yes / current backend no, because the filesystem full-scans flat directories and whole logs and has no edge table.
Several components are flagged as dormant or half-built: the Supabase/pgvector resolver, find_relations, the history projection at scale, the marks/findings layer, and cross-process seq uniqueness.
standouts: Only FilesystemResolver exists even though the constitution says the store is filesystem-first, Supabase later.
The vector store is the same filesystem, not a separate vector database, with one JSON file per (item, space).
The history projection already frames itself as the intended replacement for episodic-memory.
The verdict cleanly separates merge-ready architecture from unready filesystem implementation.
75,374 raw exchanges would imply roughly 75k objects, 75k refs, and potentially 300k–450k vector files in flat directories.
conventions: Classifies evidence inline as Observed, Inferred, or Verified.
Cites absolute paths with line anchors.
Uses a TL;DR followed by numbered sections, evidence, verdict, and flagged dormant items.
Repeats design intent from AGENTS.md and code comments to ground claims.
Maintains a neutral, non-ranking tone throughout.
Relies on content-addressed immutability, append-only logs, and mutable ref pointers.
Records are open dicts with explicit filter-key fields intended to map cleanly to SQL WHERE clauses.
concerns: Read paths full-scan flat directories and parse entire *.jsonl files, which will degrade at 75k+ records.
events.jsonl is re-parsed on every corpus listing, becoming a full-table-scan-per-query at scale.
objects/, refs/, and vectors/ are flat directories with no sharding.
Event seq assignment is not cross-process-unique, which matters for high-volume bedrock ingest.
There is no dedicated relation edge table; edges would live in marks/findings logs.
The 384-dim episodic vectors are dimensionally incompatible with the Company's 1024-dim retrieval.
The Supabase/Postgres/pgvector backend is named but unimplemented.
find_relations is explicitly described as a separate pass not built in this lane.
notes: A scale-read of the Company store that finds the addressing and record architecture merge-ready, but the filesystem backend unfit for 75k raw exchanges until a Postgres/pgvector resolver is built.
questions: What is the measured wall-time of a full events.jsonl or vectors/ scan at 75k–450k records?
Is the Supabase/Postgres resolver sketched anywhere outside this read's scope?
How exactly will session/round/project lineage be recovered from episodic-memory's per-session/per-project archive?
What is the migration/backfill plan for the 75,374 exchanges and their re-embedding?
Will the 384-dim episodic vectors be discarded or preserved in a parallel 384-dim space?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to decide whether the Company store can be the single store-of-record for the merge; the file does exactly that, walking the backend, schema, vectors, generated layers, and scaling limits to reach a verdict.
apparent_themes: Content-addressed immutability as the trust base
Backend indirection through the Resolver Protocol
Explicit filter-key fields anticipating a SQL backend
Fail-loud lineage gating for cross-session corroboration
Honest partialness rather than fabricated vectors
intention_signals: It is trying to validate whether the existing Company storage substrate can absorb the episodic-memory conversation bedrock as-is, and to argue that the schema is ready but the physical backend must be extended to Postgres/pgvector before the merge.
novelty: It treats a filesystem-backed addressed store as a deliberate Phase 1 substrate explicitly designed to be swapped for a SQL/pgvector backend without changing the address grammar or record schema.
contribution: A precise, evidence-classified inventory of the Company store's backend, schema, vector storage, and generated-content layers; a scaling assessment that separates architecture readiness from filesystem-backend limitations; a clear merge recommendation to keep the address grammar and record schema while building a Postgres/pgvector resolver and re-embedding the bedrock under the lineage gate; and a list of dormant components that need implementation.
summary_for_embedding: A readiness read evaluating whether the Company store, a filesystem-backed addressed store with JSON objects, mutable refs, append-only logs, and per-space vector JSON files, can be the single store-of-record for merging 75,374 raw episodic exchanges and their generated corpus, concluding the architecture is ready but the filesystem backend requires a Postgres/pgvector resolver.
intention_for_embedding: To determine if the Company store can absorb the episodic-memory conversation bedrock without rebuilding its record schema, and to flag the missing Postgres/pgvector backend and re-embedding work needed first.
